summary: I live in the United States, a country that often talks about a homogenous American cultural ideal but also embraces the multicultural histories and immigrant origins of its people. My own story is part of this complex mix, as I navigate being biracial and multicultural in 21st century America. The United States has shaped much of my identity and family history, from my grandparents' immigration to my father's activism during the civil rights movement.

I was born in Minneapolis, Minnesota, in 1968, and much of my life has been rooted here. Minneapolis is where I went to South High School and where I unexpectedly met my half-brother John. It’s also where my family has lived and worked, including my grandmother who was a nurse manager in Minnesota’s state mental hospitals.

Minnesota is home to much of my family’s story. My grandmother Pauline Hunter worked as a nurse manager in the state mental hospitals here, and my family has deep roots in the area. Growing up in Minnesota shaped my experiences, from school to family life, and it’s where many of my relatives still live.

When Barack Obama was elected President, it felt like a moment where the nation claimed to have transcended race, though his identity was still questioned. Like Obama, I have a mixed Caucasian and African heritage and was raised in a white family culture. His story resonated with me because it reflected some of the complexities I’ve experienced around race and identity in America.

My mom, Helen Hunter, is a family biographer who published a memoir in 2012 about her childhood and our family’s immigrant history. She has been a huge influence on me, exploring our family’s struggles and triumphs, from her father’s immigration to her mother’s fight for independence and education. Helen’s work helped me understand the complexities of our family story and my own identity.

My grandmother, Pauline Hunter, was the eldest daughter of German farmers and spoke German until it was banned during World War I. She was intelligent and tall, and she worked as a nurse manager in Minnesota’s state mental hospitals. Pauline inspired my mother and was a key figure in our family’s history, embodying resilience through times of anti-immigrant sentiment.

World War I had a direct impact on my family, especially my grandmother Pauline Hunter, whose first language German was banned in her community during the anti-immigrant sentiment of the war. This period shaped the environment in which she grew up and influenced our family’s cultural identity in America.

My grandfather Robert Hunter emigrated from County Mayo, Ireland, in 1922 after the Irish Civil War. He had been a Black and Tan during the conflict, which was a turbulent time leading to Ireland’s partition. In America, he worked alongside my grandmother in mental hospitals and trained greyhounds, contributing to our family’s immigrant story.

County Mayo in Ireland is where my grandfather Robert Hunter came from before emigrating to the United States in 1922. It was a place deeply affected by the Irish Civil War, which shaped his early life and decisions. His roots there are an important part of my family’s heritage.

Ireland is central to my family’s history through my grandfather Robert Hunter, who left during the aftermath of the Irish Civil War. The conflict and its consequences, including his involvement with the Black and Tans, influenced his journey to America and our family’s story. Ireland’s history is a thread that connects me to a larger narrative of struggle and migration.

The Irish Civil War was a defining event for my grandfather Robert Hunter, who was involved as a Black and Tan during the conflict. This war led to the partition of Ireland and ultimately influenced his decision to emigrate to the United States in 1922. The war’s legacy is part of my family’s complex history.

My grandfather Robert Hunter was a Black and Tan during the Irish Civil War, a paramilitary force involved in the conflict that led to Ireland’s partition. His involvement with this group was part of the turbulent history that shaped his life before he emigrated to America. This aspect of his past is a significant piece of my family’s story.

My birth father, Rex Mhiripiri, came from Zimbabwe, a country torn by an oppressive apartheid-style government. He was a war refugee who emigrated to the United States, and although he left my mother years ago, I later met his son, my brother John, in Minneapolis. Zimbabwe’s difficult history is part of my broader family narrative.

Apartheid, or an apartheid-style government, was the oppressive system in Zimbabwe that my birth father fled as a war refugee. This political and social turmoil forced him to leave his homeland and start a new life in the United States. The legacy of apartheid is part of the backdrop to my family’s story and my own identity.

I attended Minneapolis South High School, where I had a surprising encounter with my half-brother John, the son of my birth father. This chance meeting opened my eyes to the many siblings I have living in suburban Minneapolis. South High School was not just a place of education but also a site of family discovery.

My dad, Max Deal, moved from North Carolina to Minnesota to work in the civil rights movement. His background from North Carolina and his activism deeply influenced my upbringing and values. Even after moving here, his roots in North Carolina remained an important part of who he was.

My father, Max Deal, was involved in the civil rights movement when he moved from North Carolina to Minnesota. His commitment to social justice shaped my childhood and encouraged me to pursue academic excellence and sports. The civil rights movement was a significant influence on my family and my own identity.

Brian McCaffrey is my stepfather, who married my mother after her divorce. He is a Lutheran minister from Robbinsdale, Minnesota, with Scottish and Irish roots. Brian is a genuine person with a good sense of humor and a heart of gold, and he has been part of my evolving family story.

Robbinsdale, Minnesota, is where my stepfather Brian McCaffrey was raised. It’s part of my extended family’s geography and history, connecting to his Scottish and Irish heritage. Robbinsdale represents another thread in the complex tapestry of places that have shaped my family.
